perturbation.original_text: 2. Disclosure 2.1 In consideration for the Agency supplying Proprietary Information to the Contractor, the Contr actor shall a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to Authorised Persons and to no other person; b) ensure that only Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information to the extent strictly necessar y for the Authorised Purpose and for no other purpose. 2.2 In consideration for the Contractor supplying Proprietary Information to the Agency the Agency shall: a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to the Agency Authorised Persons and to no other person ; and b) ensure that only the Agency Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information to the extent strictly necessary for the Authorised Purpose and for no other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Disclosure 2.1 In consideration for the Agency supplying Proprietary Information to the Contractor, the Contr actor shall a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to Authorised Persons and to no other person; b) ensure that only Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information. 2.2 In consideration for the Contractor supplying Proprietary Information to the Agency the Agency shall: a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to the Agency Authorised Persons and to no other person ; and b) ensure that only the Agency Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'to the extent strictly necessary for the Authorised Purpose and for no other purpose' has been removed from sections 2.1 b) and 2.2 b). This creates an in-text contradiction because the definition of 'Authorised Purpose' still exists in Section 1.1, implying limitations on the use of Proprietary Information. However, Sections 2.1 and 2.2 now lack this restriction. This contradiction makes it unclear whether the Authorised Persons and Agency Authorised Persons can use the information without restrictions, making enforcement of the original intent ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1. Definitions 1.1 “Agency Authorised Persons” means those persons named by the Agency who have agreed to be bound by the terms of this agreement; “Authorised Persons” means those persons named by the Contractor who have agreed to be bound by the terms of this agr eement; “Authorised Purpose” means the work to be performed under the Contract; “Disclose” means the distribution, circulation, supply or communication of information or Documentation either by means of verbal, electronic or hardcopy means; 3“Documentation” means all media on which information or data of any description is recorded including all paper documents and electronic communications whether in electronic or hard copy form; “Intellectual Property Rights” means all registered intellectual property rights and unregistered intellectual property rights granted by law including all patents, trade marks, design rights, data base rights, topography rights, know -how and trade secrets or equivalent rights or rights of action anywhere in the world; “Proprietary Information” means any information the provider states to be sensitive, confidential or for restricted use together with all Documentation marked “Proprietary Information”.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that Proprietary Information should only be used 'to the extent strictly necessary for the Authorised Purpose and for no other purpose'. The changed text omits this restriction. This directly contradicts section 1.1, the 'Definitions' section, because 'Authorised Purpose' is still defined, creating an ambiguity. If the Authorised Purpose is still valid, then removing the restriction contradicts this purpose because the Agency Authorised Persons and Authorised persons can now use the Proprietary Information with no restrictions, making the section unenforceable and creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Storage Both the Agency and the Contractor shall: a) keep all Proprietary Information supplied by the other party (and all results, information, data or documentation based thereon) separate f rom all other records at its usual place of business; b) not use, reproduce, transform, or store any of the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party in an externally accessible computer or electronic information retrieval system or transmit it in a ny form or by any means whatsoever outside its usual place of business; c) keep a written record of the physical and electronic location of all Proprietary Information used by Authorised Persons and Agency Authorised Persons, a record of when and how many cop ies are made and destroy copies which are unused; d) take every reasonable precaution and step to ensure that the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party is secure and protected from unauthorised access and use.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Storage Both the Agency and the Contractor shall: a) keep all Proprietary Information supplied by the other party separate f rom all other records; b) not use, reproduce, transform, or store any of the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party in an externally accessible computer or electronic information retrieval system or transmit it in a ny form or by any means whatsoever outside; c) keep a record of when and how many cop ies are made and destroy copies which are unused; d) take every reasonable precaution and step to ensure that the Proprietary Information supplied by the other party is secure and protected from unauthorised access and use.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'at its usual place of business' from section 3 a) and 'outside its usual place of business' from section 3 b), and 'written record of the physical and electronic location of all Proprietary Information used by Authorised Persons and Agency Authorised Persons, a' from 3 c), the contract now contradicts itself. The original intention was to limit storage and usage to the usual place of business, providing a degree of control. Now, the location is not defined, creating an internal contradiction of where this information can be saved. The omission of a detailed tracking of where the information is saved or shared, causes the uncertainty if section 4 can be fullfilled, about returning the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Further Assurances 4.1 At the request of the supplier of the Proprietary Information the receiving party shall immediately return all Proprietary Information which the receiving party (or an Authorised Person/Agency Authorised Person) has in its possession, custody or control including all materials (in hardcopy or electronic form) that incorporate any part of the Proprietary Information and, if required, destroy any copies of such Proprietary Information and materials and certify in writing that such destruction has been completed.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'at its usual place of business' from section 3 a) and 'outside its usual place of business' from section 3 b), and 'written record of the physical and electronic location of all Proprietary Information used by Authorised Persons and Agency Authorised Persons, a' from 3 c), creates a contradiction with section 4.1. Section 4.1 requires the receiving party to return 'all Proprietary Information which the receiving party... has in its possession, custody or control'. The removal of the location constraints in section 3 and the removal of a detailed tracking of where the information is saved or shared means that the receiving party may not know where all the information is stored, making it difficult to comply with section 4.1's obligation to return all Proprietary Information. This aligns with the definition of Omissions – In Text Contradiction, where essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Notices 5.1 Notification under this agreement should b e sent to: Name Address Telephone Fax e-mail For the Agency For the Contractor 5.2 Notification sent under this agreement shall be deemed valid and effective when received by mail, fax or e -mail providi ng there is written confirmation of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Notices 5.1 Notification under this agreement should b e sent to: Name Address Telephone Fax e-mail For the Agency For the Contractor 5.2 Notification sent under this agreement shall be deemed valid and effective when received by mail, fax or e -mail.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'providing there is written confirmation of receipt' from section 5.2, this creates an in-text contradiction. While the contract still mandates notices be sent via specific channels (mail, fax, email), the requirement for written confirmation of receipt is removed. This leads to potential disputes about whether a notification was effectively delivered, as the sender no longer needs to prove receipt. The removal of confirmation receipt contradicts the intention of ensuring that the other party acknowledges receiving important information, making Section 6 on Termination ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Termination 6.1 The Agency may terminate this agreement at any time by notice in writing if; a) the Contractor becomes insolvent, ceases to carry out its business, has a receiver, liquidator, administrative r eceiver, administrator, trustee or other similar officer appointed over the whole or part of its assets or an order is made or a resolution passed for the winding up of the Contractor (save for a solvent winding up as part of a bona fide reconstruction or amalgamation); or b) the Contractor commits a breach of this agreement or of the Contract which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 60 days of notice to the Agency. 6.2 The Contractor may termi nate this agreement if the Contract is cancelled by the Agency for some reason other than the fault of the Contractor.
perturbation.justification: The original text in section 5.2 required 'written confirmation of receipt' for notifications sent by mail, fax, or email to be considered valid. The changed text removes this requirement. Section 6.1 allows the Agency to terminate the agreement if the Contractor commits a breach 'of which notice is given.' By removing the written confirmation requirement, it becomes more difficult to prove that notice was effectively given, potentially leading to disputes and impacting the Agency's ability to terminate the agreement under 6.1. This is because the 'notice' might be disputed and difficult to prove, directly contradicting the implication in section 6.1 that effective notice must occur to allow for termination. The change therefore creates an in-text contradiction and falls under Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Termination 6.1 The Agency may terminate this agreement at any time by notice in writing if; a) the Contractor becomes insolvent, ceases to carry out its business, has a receiver, liquidator, administrative r eceiver, administrator, trustee or other similar officer appointed over the whole or part of its assets or an order is made or a resolution passed for the winding up of the Contractor (save for a solvent winding up as part of a bona fide reconstruction or amalgamation); or b) the Contractor commits a breach of this agreement or of the Contract which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 60 days of notice to the Agency. 6.2 The Contractor may termi nate this agreement if the Contract is cancelled by the Agency for some reason other than the fault of the Contractor.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Termination 6.1 The Agency may terminate this agreement at any time by notice in writing if; a) the Contractor becomes insolvent, ceases to carry out its business, has a receiver, liquidator, administrative r eceiver, administrator, trustee or other similar officer appointed over the whole or part of its assets or an order is made or a resolution passed for the winding up of the Contractor (save for a solvent winding up as part of a bona fide reconstruction or amalgamation); or b) the Contractor commits a breach of this agreement or of the Contract. 6.2 The Contractor may termi nate this agreement if the Contract is cancelled by the Agency.
perturbation.explanation: The omission of 'which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 60 days of notice to the Agency' from section 6.1 b) makes it possible to interpret that the contract can be terminated for any minor breach of the agreement, that can be fixed. This creates a direct contradiction with the principle of good faith and reasonable interpretation, making Section 7.1 regarding liability much more uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Miscellaneous 7.1 Both the Agency and the Contractor make no representation and give no warranty concerning the accuracy, format (e ither in hard copy or electronic form) suitability or fitness for purpose of the Proprietary Information supplied under this agreement. Neither the Agency nor the Contractor shall be liable in any form for any loss, damage or expenses incurred an Authorise d Person or Agency Authorised Person which result either directly or indirectly from the accessing, using, distributing, circulating, supplying or copying the Proprietary Information supplied under this agreement unless 6such loss, damage or expense cannot be excluded by agreement under the governing law of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits 'which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 60 days of notice to the Agency' from section 6.1 b). This makes contract termination possible for any minor breach, contradicting the principle of good faith and reasonable interpretation. Section 7.1 disclaims liability for various information-related losses unless such exclusion is prohibited by law. The original text in 6.1 limited termination to material breaches or unremedied breaches. Removing this limitation expands the scope of potential breaches leading to termination, increasing potential liabilities for the contractor and creating conflict with section 7.1, which limits liabilities.

Agency Employees Name Signature Address Date 8 Agency Third Parties Name Signature Address Date Approved Purpose The Agency agrees to supply Proprietary Information for the sole purpose of completing [ ].
perturbation.changed_text: Appendix 1 Authorised Persons The following individuals named by the Contractor have read and understood the terms and conditions of this Non -Disclosure Agreement dated [ ] and their signature confirms agreement to be bound by its terms. Contractor Employees Name Signature Address Date Contractor Third Parties Name Signature Address Date Agency Authorised Persons The following individuals named by the Agency have read and understood the terms and conditions of this Non -Disclosure Agreement dated [ ] and their signature confirms agreement to be bound by its terms. Agency Employees Name Signature Address Date 8 Agency Third Parties Name Signature Address Date
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Approved Purpose The Agency agrees to supply Proprietary Information for the sole purpose of completing [ ].' from Appendix 1, while Authorised Purpose is still being reffered in the rest of the file, creates an in-text contradiction. The contract references 'Authorised Purpose' throughout (e.g., Section 2.1, 2.2), implying a defined scope for the use of Proprietary Information. However, this scope is no longer defined because Approved Purpose was removed, which contradicts the limited usage intended in Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Disclosure 2.1 In consideration for the Agency supplying Proprietary Information to the Contractor, the Contr actor shall a) only Disclose the Proprietary Information to Authorised Persons and to no other person; b) ensure that only Authorised Persons use, Disclose or copy (in hardcopy form or electronic form) the Proprietary Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text included a definition of 'Approved Purpose,' which is removed in the changed text. Section 2.1 explicitly refers to and limits disclosure to 'Authorised Persons', predicated on the Agency supplying proprietary information based on that 'Approved Purpose'. With the removal of the 'Approved Purpose' definition in the changed text, section 2.1 creates an in-text contradiction because the scope and limitation around proprietary information are now undefined, yet section 2.1 still operates as if a defined 'Approved Purpose' exists.
